text: 10 A common saying in solvent degradation management of amine-based post combustion capture is “keep the solvent 11 clean” to save the solvent. Removal of metals, degradation products and trace reactants, such as NO 2, which are all contained in the flue gas that is passed through the absorber and are assumed to contribute to amine loss. However, in practice these lab experiments have been performed with fresh amines and do not take into account the actual structure of the solvent matrix, the interactions between potential catalysts and contaminants like degradation products and oxidizing species like NO 2, and the activation energy needed for a degradation pathway to start. This work therefore 16 investigated the CESAR1 (aqueous solution of a 3.0 molar monoethanol amine (MEA) concentration) solvent degradation 17 behavior, focusing on the evolution of carbamates as well as nitriles in the presence of several dissolved iron species and atmospheric 18 oxygen. These parameters were selected as iron and oxygen are present in higher concentrations in the overall process loop.
